text: FELIX, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Almeria, at the foot of the Sierra Almejijar. Pop. 2,122. It has a parish church.
FELIX-BANYA, an establishment of mineral baths in Hungary, in the comitat of Bahar, 5 m. SE of Grosswardein.
FELIX-BOOTHIA. See BOOTHIA-FELIX.
FELIX (CAPE), or UJONG SARRY, a headland of the island of Sumatra, on the NW coast, in N lat. 3° 40′, and E long. 91° 10′.
FELIX (CAPE), a promontory on the N coast of King William land, in Arctic America, in N lat. 69° 53', W long. 97° 52'.
FELIX HARBOUR, an inlet on the SE coast of Boothia Felix, in Arctic America, in N lat. 69° 58' 42", W long. 92° 1' 6". Capt. Ross wintered here in 1829–30.

FELIX (ISLAND), a small island in the South Pacific, about 550 m. from the coast of Chili, 20 m. WNW of Ambrose island, in S lat. 26° 25', and W long. 79° 30'. It is distinguished by a remarkable rock at its NW extremity, which has the appearance from nearly every point of view of a vessel in full sail.
FELIX-KIRK, or FELIS-KIRK, a parish and township in the N. R. of Yorkshire. Area of p., 6,990 acres. Pop. 931. The township is 4 m, NE of Thirsk. Area 1,010 acres. Pop. 119.

FELIX (SAINT), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Garonne, cant. of Revel, 12 m. ENE of Ville-Franche-de-Lauragais. Pop. 2,618. The town is substantially built of stone, and has an old castle, a fine promenade on the bank of the river, and a handsome hall.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Lot, cant. of Figeac. Pop. 1,071.

FELIX (SAINT), a cape on the W coast of the island of Madagascar, in the Buques territory, 75 m. N of the bay of St. Augustine, in S lat. 22° 30'. It forms the most westerly point of the island. FELIX (SAINT), FELLIS, or RAS-EL-FIL, a promontory of Eastern Africa, on the Somanli coast, 20 m. WNW of Cape Guardafui, in N lat. 12°. It was the Elephas Promontorium of the ancients.

FELIX-DE-SORGUE (SAINT), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Camaris, 9 m. SE of St. Affrique, on the r. bank of the Sorgue. Pop. 1,891. It has a manufactory of cloth and a wool-spinning-mill.
FELIXSTOW, a parish in Suffolk, 11 m. SE of Ipswich, at the mouth of the Deben. Area 1,170 acres. Pop. 552.

FELIZZANO, a mandemento and town of Piedmont, in the prov. and 9 m. W of Alexandria, near the l. bank of the Tanaro. Pop. 2,175. FELK, Fulka, or Folk, a free royal town of Hungary, in the comitat of Zips, near the l. bank of the Poprad, and about a mile NW of the town of that name. Pop. 1,495. It has a Catholic and a Lutheran church; and possesses several manufactories of linen and lace, and distilleries.
FELKINGTON, a township in the p. of Norham, co. of Durham, 12 m. NNW of Wooler, Northumberland. Area 1,400 acres. Pop. 141.
FELKIRK, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. NNE of Barnesley, intersected by the York and Derby railway. Area 6,360 acres. Pop. 1,186.
FELL, a village of Illyria, in the gov. of Laybach, circle and 29 m. WNW of Villach, on the r. bank of the Drave. It has some iron-works, and in the vicinity are mines of silver and arsenic.

FELLA, a river which has its source in the Carnatic Alps, in Illyria, runs first N, then W, enters Venetian Lombardy, bends SW, and falls into the Tagliamento, on the l. bank, 11 m. NW of Venzone, after an impetuous course of about 39 m.
FELLA, or CETRARO (CAPO), a headland of Naples, on the W coast of the prov. of Calabria Citra, 6 m. NW of Cetraro, in N lat. 39° 31', and E long. 15° 55'.
FELLABRUNN (NIEDER), a town of Austria, in the prov. of Lower Austria, circle of Korneuburg, 20 m. N of Vienna. Pop. 630.
FELLABRUNN (OBER), a town of Austria, in the prov. of Lower Austria, circle of Lower Mannhartsberg, E of Meissau.
FELLACH, a village of Austria, in Carinthia, in the gov. of Laybach and circle of Klagenfurt. At has acidulous springs and baths. FELLAHS. See article EGYPT.

FELLATAHS, or Fulans, a people of the NW region of Africa. From Bornu to the shores of Senegambia, and from the boundary of the Zahara to the mountains of Guinea, are scattered a number of tribes belonging to this race, and mentioned by travellers under the several appellations of Fulahs, Foules, Fuli, Pholey, Fouls, Foulaues, Fellaus, and Fellatahs. The tract of country which they inhabit extends over 28 degrees by 7 degrees, and presents a surface of 60,000 square leagues. These tribes formerly lived isolated in forests, leading a pastoral life; but at a later period, several of them, under the inspiration of Islamism, founded independent states in the midst of the Negro populations. Some of these states in Senegambia are contiguous to the French and English settlements, and have had frequent relations with Europeans. Their original capital appears to be Timbu, in the country of Futadiallo, near the sources of the Rio-Grande. In Kaorma, at the close of the last cent., the F. tribes took up arms at the voice of a warlike prophet, made rapid conquest of the neighbouring provinces, and established a formidable empire in Nigritia, possessing a kind of supremacy over every other nation of the same race. It was by the chief of this empire, the Sultan Bello, a remarkable character and a learned Mussulman, that Clapperton was entertained in both his voyages. The Fellatahs are so named by the Negroes, but they themselves use the term Fellan, or more exactly Faulan; but as this nation carries away Negroes for the purposes of slavery, Fellatah appears to be a term of reproach, like that of Serdu by which the Negroes designate the Tuaricks. At Senegal, and on the borders of the Gambia, they are called Fulah and Puhls. Mungo Park designates them under the first of these names, and Mollien under the second. Hodgson contends that the Fellatahs are neither of Arabian nor of Berber origin, but have descended from the elevated plateau where the Niger takes its origin. As the Fellatahs border on Abyssinia, it is probable that they have some relation with the Fallashas of that country. Bruce says that the latter are Jews, and speak the ancient Ethiopian language, but this language is little known. An examination of the language of Tibbu, Bornu, Haussa, and Timbuctu, proves that they have no declensions either for the genders or for the plural number. Perhaps even their verbs are not conjugated. If we compared the language of the Tuaricks who inhabit the N, and that of the Fellatahs to the S, with the simple and uncultivated idiom of Sudan, perhaps we should find that there exists as much difference between the languages of Africa, as between the colours of its inhabitants; and that, like them, they may be divided into white and black. The social character of this people is evidently quite distinct from that of the Negroes. No Negro people has ever been discovered of pastoral and nomadic habits, conquerors and propagators of a religious creed. Nor are their physical characteristics less distinct. The F. of pure descent are tall and thin in figure, their faces are oval, the nose is aquiline, and the hair is glossy; their skin is tanned but not black, and the extremities of their limbs are small and slender. Lander was struck by the resemblance which the F. bore to the Caffres near Graham's Town in S. Africa. Notwithstanding palpable differences, however, the F. have always been classed with the Negro races, of which they have been looked upon as a variety. This apparent anomaly induced M. D'Eichthal to undertake an inquiry into the history and origin of the F. Convinced, by a comparison of their generic characteristics, that they could not belong to the Negro race, -perceiv- FEL African tribes,—he was led to inquire whether they might not have some relation with the swarthy inhabitants of the Indian archipelago, the Malays, whose presence had been traced even in the population of Madagascar. It is well-known that the inhabitants of the extended and numerous isles of that Archipelago are divided into two principal races, one black, the other swarthy; and, although the latter is far from being homogeneous, nevertheless the several dialects spoken by it in many points are so strikingly similar, that they can only be considered as belonging to one family; this affinity, too, extends to the east as far as Polynesia, which may be looked upon as the great nursery of the civilization of the Archipelago; and to the W, as far as the island of Madagascar, hitherto held to be the most westerly station of the Malayan race. The results of M. D'Eichthal's investigations are as follow:-On comparing the F. language with the group of languages spoken in the Archipelago, it was found to bear many and striking affinities to all of them, but not to one in particular; for here we may apply Crawford's observation on the language of Madagascar-"that it presents no special analogy with any one of the present languages of the Archipelago, and that its formation must have originated in a state of things far different from the present." Nevertheless this remark, says M. D'Eichthal, as regards the F. language, is liable to some exceptions. The Java tongue presents more numerous and more particular similitudes with it than the other dialects of the Archipelago. From this tongue appears to have been derived a certain number of Sanscrit words found in the F. language; nor is this circumstance the sole proof of the indirect connexion, at least, of this nation with India. To this connexion the F. are indebted for their breed of oxen (zebus), and the cowries which are in use among them, and which travellers have been surprised to find in W. Africa. The presence of the zebus and cowries in this region always coincides with that of the F.; with them they make their appearance, and with them they disappear, so that nothing can be more natural than to attribute their introduction to them. The languages of the Archipelago, moreover, afford an explanation of the national name: Foulah, in the Rotte dialect; Foute, in that of Madagascar: Poutch, in that of Java, signifying white. Probably, when the F. settled in Africa they assumed the name of 'white,' to distinguish themselves from the surrounding tribes. According to a very general tradition, they ascribe their origin to a race of white men, and are particularly proud of this descent, which distinguishes them from the blacks, with whom, up to the period of their conquest, they evinced great repugnance to form any alliance. In what manner or at what time they passed into Africa it is impossible to ascertain; in all probability, however, their emigration must have been anterior to the use of the plough and the alphabet in the Archipelago, for with neither of these are they acquainted. Perhaps the narrow weavingloom used in the NW of Africa, by this tribe as well as by the neighbouring Negroes, may be attributed to them. This loom recalls that of similar dimensions used in the Carolina islands, and which, no doubt, preceded in the Archipelago the use of the more improved loom subsequently introduced by the Hindus. The word foulah, signifying the art of weaving, is used in the same signification in the Archipelago. It may be suggested that the F. are the same people who are designated in the ethnological table of the book of Genesis under the name of Fhout or Pout, and placed after Kush and Misraim. This supposition, if it be exact, would ascribe their presence in Africa to times of the remotest antiquity. Their emigration must have been connected with that of the Malays of Madagascar; or, which is less probable, their relations with that island were established at a later period; for those words which are common to the two languages are not to be found elsewhere. Their first station in E. Africa appears to have been in the island of Meroe, where we find several names of F. localities, and the Dar Fur (literally 'Four country'), which derives its name from them. Hence they spread themselves successively over the whole of NW. Africa, where, as we know, they have recently established a vast empire. The researches of M. D'Eichthal, in a scientific point of view, appear to establish a distinction between two races, hitherto more or less confounded; but Dr. Pritchard is still of opinion that the F. are a genuine African race. The manners of the F. are thus described by Clapperton: "The dress of the men is a red cap with a blue tassel of silk, a white turban, part of which, or a fold, shades the brow and eyes; another fold is taken over the nose, which covers the mouth and chin, hanging down on the breast; a white shirt, close at the breast, and short in the skirt, a large white tobe and white trousers, trimmed with red or green silk, and a pair of sandals or boots; this is the dress of the greater part of the wealthy inhabitants. When travelling, they wear over the turban a broadbrimmed straw hat with a round low crown. Some who do not affect great sanctity or learning, wear check tobes and blue turbans over the forehead, with the end hanging down behind; the poorer, a white check tobe, white cap and trousers, and sandals. Some are content with the straw hat only; but all wear a sword, which is carried over the left shoulder. The women have a cloth striped with blue, white, and red, falling as low as the ankles; silver rings in the ears, about an inch and a half in diameter; bracelets of horn, glass, brass, copper, or silver, according to the quality of the wearer; round the neck, beads and strings of glass or coral; round the ankles, brass, copper, or silver, and sometimes rings on the toes as well as fingers. The fashionable ornament is a Spanish dollar soldered fast to a ring. The poor women have pewter, ing, moreover, that they were in no way connected with the | brass, and copper rings. The hair is generally turned up like a crest on the top of the head, with something like a pig's tail hanging down from each extremity, before the ears. Some of the F. women have the hair frizzed out at the ends, all round the head; others have the hair plaited in four small plaits, going round the head like a riband or bandeau. This and all the plaited parts are well smeared over with indigo or shumri. The razor is applied to smooth all uneven places, and to give a high and fine arch to the forehead; they thin the eye-brows to a fine line, which, with the eye-lashes, are rubbed over with pounded lead ore; this is done by drawing a small pen that has been dipped in this ore. The teeth are then dyed with the gora-nut, and a root of a shining red colour; the hands and feet, the toe and finger nails, are stained red with henna. A lady thus equipped is fit to appear in the best society. The looking-glass is a circular piece of metal, about an inch and a half in diameter, set in a small skin box, and is often applied to. The young girls of the better sort of people dress much in the same manner as their mothers after they arrive at the age of nine or ten; before that they have very little dress, except the binta or apron. scolloped or vandyked round with red cloth, with two long broad strings vandyked round in the same manner, hanging down as low as the heels behind. This is the dress of the poorer sort of people until fit for marriage, as also of a great many of the virgin female slaves. They always bury their dead behind the house which the deceased occupied while living. The following day all the friends and relations of the deceased visit the head of the family, and sit a while with the bereaved party. If the husband dies, the widow returns to the house of her parents, with the property she brought with her. The domestic slaves are generally well treated. The hours of labour for his master are from daylight till mid-day; the remainder of the day is employed on his own, or in any other way he may think proper. At the time of harvest, when they cut and tie up the grain, each slave gets a bundle of the different sorts of grain, about a bushel of our measure, for himself. The grain on his own ground is entirely left for his own use, and he may dispose of it as he thinks proper. The children of a slave are also slaves; and when able, are usually sent out to attend the goats and sheep, and at a more advanced age, the bullocks and larger cattle. The domestic slaves are fed with the rest of the family, with whom they appear to be on an equality of footing. The male and female children of the better sort of the F. are all taught to write and read Arabic, but are instructed separately. The male children of the great are generally sent to another town, at some distance from that where their parents reside, to receive their education: in which case they usually reside in the house of a friend, and a mallem, or man of learning, attends them. Those of the middle and lower classes generally send their children to the schools, which they attend for an hour at daybreak, and another at sunset, reading their Arabic lessons aloud and simultaneously. They are required to get their lessons by heart before the writing is washed-off the board on which it is written. The ink thus diluted is drunk by the scholars, when their master writes a new lesson on the board. These Africans keep up the appearance of religion: they pray five times a-day. They seldom take the trouble to wash before prayers, except in the morning; but they go through the motions of washing, clapping their hands on the ground as if in water, and muttering a prayer. All their prayers and religious expressions are in Arabic; and I may say, without exaggeration, taking Negroes and F. together, that not one in a thousand knows what he is saying. All they know of their religion is, to repeat their prayers by rote in Arabic. Of the F., about 1 in 10 is able to read and write. They believe, they say, in predestination, but it is all a farce. They believe, however, in divination by the book, in dreams, and in good and bad omens. The government of the F. in Sudan is in its infancy. The governors of the different provinces are appointed during pleasure; and all their property, on their death or removal, falls to the sultan. The appointment to a vacancy is sold to the highest bidder, who is generally a near relation, provided that his property is sufficient to enable him to bid up to the mark. All the inferior offices in the towns are sold in like manner by the governors, who also succeed to the property of those petty officers at their death or removal. In the prov. of Kane they have no regular system of taxation, A great deal of marketable property is claimed by the governor, such as two-thirds of the produce of all the date-trees and other fruit-trees, the proprietor being allowed only the remaining third. A small duty is also levied on every article sold in the market; or, in lieu thereof, a certain rent is paid for the stall or shed. A duty is also fixed on every lobe that is dyed blue, and sold. On grain there is no duty. Kano produces the greatest revenue that the sultan receives: it is paid monthly, in horses, cloth, and cowries. Adamowa pays yearly in slaves; Yacoba, in slaves and lead-ore; Zegzeg, in slaves and cowries; Zamfra, the same, Hadiga and Katagum, and Zaonima, in horses, bullocks, and slaves; Cashna, in slaves, cowries, and cloth; Ader, or Tadela, in bullocks, sheep, camels, and a coarse kind of cotton cloth." See article GAMBIA.
FELLBACH, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Neckar, 3 m. E of Canstadt, and 5 m. ENE of Stuttgart, at an alt. of 1,026 ft. above sealevel. Pop. 2,744. The environs produce good wine.
FELLBRINGEN, or FELLERY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haut-Rhin, cant. of Saint- Marin, 21 m. NNE of Belfort. Pop. 1,591. FEL

FELLELIN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Creuse, arrond. of Aubusson. The cant. comprises 9 com. Pop. in 1831, 12,779; in 1841, 13,069. The town is 5 m. S of Aubusson, and 27 m. SE of Guéret, on the Creuse. Pop. 3,589. It has a college and an hospital; and possesses extensive manufactories of carpets and other woollen fabrics, and several dye-works and paper-mills. Fairs are held 13 times a-year.
FELLERIES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. and 4 m. ENE of Avesnes. Pop. 1,650. It has manufactories of wood-ware.
FELLEY, an extra-parochial district of Nottinghamshire, 8 m. SSW of Mansfield. Pop. 41.
FELLHEIM, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Suabia, N of Memmingen. Pop. 564.

FELLIN, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Livonia, district and 40 m. ESE of Pernau, and 123 m. NE of Riga, on the N bank of Lake Virtz- Ervé, near the egress of a small river of the same name, an affluent of the Navast. Pop. 658. It is ill-built, and has no edifice except a church worthy of notice. The origin of this town dates from a very early period. In 1224 it was fortified by the Livonians, and several times successfully resisted the attacks of the Russians. It was taken in 1482 and 1560 by the czar Ivan-Vasiliévitch, and reduced to ashes; and subsequently passed into the hands of the Poles and Swedes.
FELLINGSBRO, a district of Sweden, in the E part of the prefecture of Œrebro. It derives its name from its principal village.
FELLISCLIFFE, a township in the p. of Hampsthwaite, W. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. W of Knaresborough. Area 2,320 acres. Pop. 363.
FELLSIDE, a township in the p. of Whickham, co. Durham. Pop. 593.
FELMERSHAM, a parish in Bedfordshire, 3 m. ENE of Harold, on the Ouse. Area 2,400 acres. Pop. 531.
FELMINGHAM, a parish in Norfolk, 21 m. WSW of North Walsham, and E of the Bure. Area 2,010 acres. Pop. 408.
FELPHAM, a parish in Sussex, 6 m. SW of Arundel. Area 1,800 acres. Pop. 555.

FELSBERG, a bailiwick and town of Hesse-Cassel, in the prov. of Nieder-Hessen, circle and 5 m. WSW of Melsungen, and 13 m. SSW of Cassel, on the l. bank of the Eder. Pop. 1,233, of whom about 200 are Jews. It has a church and an hospital, and on the summit of an adjacent rocky eminence are the ruins of a fortress. Pop. of bail. 6,575.—Also a mountain of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the prov. of Starkenburg, 4 m. from Bensheim, which rises to the height of 1,546 ft. above sea-level. It is remarkable for the immense masses of granite which are found on its SE side.

FELSBURG, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, 4 m. from Chur, on the l. bank of the Rhine, opposite Ems. This v. stands directly under a precipitous mountain-wall from which isolated fragments have frequently fallen, and which seems, from its enormous gaps and clefts directly above the v., to threaten it with imminent danger.
FELSHAM, a parish in Suffolk, 7 m. SW of Stowmarket. Area 1,440 acres. Pop. 398.
FELSOF, a village of Hungary, in the comitat of Szathmar, NE of Nemethi.
FELSO-KOSZTOLANY, a village of Hungary, in the comitat of Fars, 25 m. SW of Kremnitz.
FELSTEAD, or FEELSTEAD, a parish in Essex. Area 7,170 acres. Pop. 1,798.
FELSTIN, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Podolia, district and 14 m. SW of Proskourov, and 48 m. N of Kamenetz. Pop. 680. $
FELSZTYN, a small town of Galicia, in the circle and 12 m. W of Sambor, and 19 m. SSE of Przemysl, on the l. bank of the Strwiaz.
FELTHAM, a parish in Middlesex, 31 m: SW of Hounslow. Area 2,620 acres. Pop. 1,029.
FELTHORPE, a parish in Norfolk, 7 m. NNW of Norwich, and E of the Wensum. Area 2,250 acres. Pop. in 1831, 502; in 1841, 574.

FELTON, a parish of Herefordshire, 72 m. NNE of Hereford. Area 990 acres. Pop. 113.-Also a parish and township in Northumberland, 9 m. S of Alnwick, on the Coquet, which is here crossed by a good stone bridge. Area of p. 13,950 acres. Pop. 1,585. Pop. of township 623.—Also a tything in the p. of Winford, Somerset. Pop. 246. See also WHITCHURCH.
FELTON-BUTLER, a township in the p. of Great-Ness, Salop. Pop. 65.
FELTON-WEST, a parish and township in Salop, 45 m. SE of Oswestry, and intersected by the Llanymynich canal. Area of p. 6,160 acres. Pop. 1,087. Pop. of township 214.

FELTRE, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, deleg. and 18 m. SW of Belluno, and 48 m. NNW of Venice, near the l. bank of the Colineda, 4 m. above the confluence with the Piave, and at an alt. of 1,033 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 5,500. It has some fortifications, and is generally well-built. Its streets are broad, and well-paved; and it has a handsome square, a cathedral, several churches, 2 convents, a gymnasium, an hospital, and some other educational and charitable institutions. Silk-spinning, wax-bleaching, and the manufacture of leather, form the principal branches of local industry. The trade consists chiefly in silk, wine, and oil.—The title of Duke of Feltre was conferred by Napoleon in 1809, on General Clarke.
FELTRINO, a river of Naples, which descends from Monte Erminio, and flows into the Adriatic to the N of Lanciano.
FELTRONE, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the deleg. and 33 m. NW of Udine, district and 2 m. E of Ampezzo, in the valley of the Tagliamento.
FELTWILL ST. MARY AND ST. NICHOLAS, a parish in Norfolk, 6 m. SSE of Stoken-Ferry, and N of the Brandon. Area 14,810 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,231; in 1841, 1,512.
FELTZ, a town of Belgium, in the prov. and 14 m. NNE of Luxemburg, cant. and 6 m. ENE of Mersch, on the l. bank of the Erens. Pop. 885. It has some manufactories of common cloth.

FELUJAH, a village of Asiatic Turkey, on the E bank of the Euphrates, 140 m. below Hit, near the remains of the ancient Ambar or Perisabur. The river is here crossed by a bridge of boats 150 yds. in length; and about midway between F. and Musseyib, 74 m. further down the stream, is the ferry of Macdam, the nearest point to Bagdad. The Beduins cultivate a considerable tract above and below F., in which dates, grapes, grain, and cotton, are produced.

FELUJE, or Peluch, a small island in the NW part of the gulf of Persia, near the coast of Arabia, 10 m. E of Koueit. FELUPES, FALUPPOS, or FLUPS, a people of W. Africa, whose territory lies to the S of the mouth of the Gambia, and to the N of that of the St. Domingo river, along the Casamanza and its various branches; and extends about 150 m. inland. They are supposed to number 50,000, inhabit about 60 or 70 villages, and seem to be governed by different independent chiefs. Golberry says: "The F. go nearly naked, except a very small apron. They bind the upper part of the arms and the part near the wrist, the upper part of the thighs, the knees, the legs, and the ankles, with laces of leather so tight that the intermediate parts are unnaturally distended. They scarify their face and body, and imprint on them all kinds of fantastic figures. Their hair is very woolly and carly, but longer than that of Negroes in general: they gather it together on the top of the head into a tuft, which FEN stands erect, and is 5 or 6 inches in length. Their beard also, which they let grow, they collect and twist so that it projects many inches from the chin. They are covered with grigris or charms. Their colour is a deep black, but their skin is rough. Their features are tolerably regular, and have more affinity with those of the blacks of India than with those of the Negroes. In stature they are small and short, but are strong and nimble runners. Their physiognomy is gloomy, and they are taciturn in their manners. They converse but little with their neighbours, and they are very jealous of their women, who, however, are not handsome. They always carry quivers filled with poisoned arrows; these quivers are placed transversely on their backs. fastened to a strong leathern shoulder-belt. In their left hand they hold a bow, 6 ft. in length, and they carry likewise 4 or 5 lances, which they throw with great dexterity. Although savage, dull, and incommunicative, their neighbours do not complain of them, and the F. pass for good people; but they are warlike, and, if offended, avenge themselves with ferocity. Though the F. communicate little with the factories on the Gambia, yet their intercourse is very considerable with the Portuguese establishments on the Casamanza and on the other rivers to the S, as far as the Nuno-Tristao; and I have been assured that these Negroes frequent them habitually and familiarly."
FELUY, a commune and v. of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, arrond. and 16 m. NW of Charleroi. Pop. 1,917. There are valuable stone-quarries in this com.
FELVINZ, a town of Transylvania, near the Maros, 27 m. S of Clausenburg.

FEMEAS, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, comarca of Rio-de-São-Francisco, rising in the Serrada-Tabatonga, and joining the Rio-Grande, after a course of about 30 m., between the confluence of the Mosquito, and of the Rio-das-Ondas, with the Rio- Grande.

FEMERN, or FEHMARN, a small island of Denmark, in the prov. of Sleswig, situated in the Baltic, opposite to the coast of Holstein, from which it is separated by the Femer-sund, a channel about 1 m. broad. Its circuit is about 30 m.; area 70 sq. m. Pop. in 1846, 8,600, in 1,380 families. Its surface is low and level. Part of it is under tillage, another part affords good pasturage. It exported 6,021 tons of wheat in 1838, and 3,325 tons of barley; besides butter, cheese, lard, and fresh and cured provisions. Fishing is the chief means of subsistence to the inhabitants; some of the women are employed in knitting stockings. Burg, the chief town, is in N lat. 54° 26' 10". Pop. 1,800.—The other two chief villages are Petersdorf and Puttgarden.
FEMINA, a small island in the Mediterranean, under Capo-di-Gallo, on the N coast of Sicily, 9 m. NNW of Palermo.
FEMME-OSAGE, a township of St. Charles co., in Missouri, U. S., 82 m. E of Jefferson, on the N bank of the Missouri. Pop. 1,358.
FEMOE, a small island of Denmark, between Sieland and Laaland.

FENAGH, a parish in co. Leitrim, 2 m. SSW of Ballinamore. Area 9,764 acres. Pop. in 1831, 4,172; in 1841, 4,426.—Also a p. in co. Carlow, consisting of several detached portions. Area 10,521 acres. Pop. 4,314. The church is 5 m. E by S of Leighlinbridge.—Also a v. in co. Limerick, 6 m. E by S of Newcastle.
FENAIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. and 9 m. E of Douay. Pop. 1,942.
FENCOT, a hamlet in the p. of Charlton-Otmoor, co. of Oxford, 4 m. SW of Bicester. Area 750 acres. Pop. in 1841, 144.
FENDEKLI, a town of Turkey, in Romelia, on the r. bank of the Tondja, 20 m. N by E of Tchirmen.
FENELON, a township and v. in Upper Canada, skirting on Sturgeon lake.—The v. is situated in the NE part of the township, 25 m. N of Lindsay.
FENERA, a small island in the gulf of Venice, near the coast of Istria, in N lat. 44° 54'.

FENESTRELLE, a strong fortress of Piedmont, on the Clusone, near the borders of Dauphiny, consisting properly of three forts, which are built on eminences, and communicate with each other by covered ways cut in the solid rock. Seen from the road which passes immediately under it, it has the appearance of prodigious strength. In the valley below lies the v. of F., 7 m. SSE of Susa, and 20 m. NNW of Pignerol. Pop. 900.

FE'NE'TRANGE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, arrond. of Sarrebourg.—The cant., comprising 21 com., had a pop. of 12,590 in 1841.—The com. and town, on the 1. bank of the Sarre, 19 m. E of Chateau-Salins, and 56 m. ENE of Nancy, at an alt. of 279 metres or 305 yds., had a pop. of 1,464 in 1841. There are tanneries, bleachfields, and oilworks here.
FENEU, a small town of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Briollay, 6 m. N of Angers. Pop. 1,331.
FENIOUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Deux-Sevres, cant. of Coulonges, 7 m. NE of Niort. Pop. 1,520.
FENIS, a town of the Sardinian states, in the prov. and 9 m. E of Aosta, on the r. bank of the Dora-Baltea.
FENIT, an island on the W coast of co. Kerry, in the bay of Tralee, 7 m. W of Tralee. Area 686 acres. Pop. 315.
FENITON, a parish in Devonshire, 31 m. WSW of Honiton. Area 1,340 acres. Pop. in 1831, 343; in 1841. 315.
FENKI, a town of Corea, 36 m. NNW of Longkuang.
FENNER, a township in Madison co., in the state of New York, U.S., 112 m. W by N of Albany, watered by Canaseraga and Chittenango creeks. Pop. 1,997.
FENNERSVILLE, a village in Monroe co., in Pennsylvania, U. S., 118 m. NE of Harrisburg.
FENNY, a river which rises in the mountains separating Bengal from Ava; forms the boundary between the districts of Tipperah and Chittagong; and falls into the sea in N lat. 22° 50'.
FENNY-STRATFORD. See STRATFORD.

FENTON, a parish in Lincolnshire, 5 m. ESE of Newark. Area 1,220 acres. Pop. in 1831, 102; in 1841, 120.—Also a township in the p. of Kettlethorpe, in the same co., 9 m. WNW of Lincoln, and E of the Trent. Pop. 254.—Also a hamlet in the p. of Sturton, Nottinghamshire. Pop. 74. - Also a township in the p. of Wooler, Northumberland. Pop. 205.—See also Faugh and Fenton and Pidley- CUM-FENTON.
FENTON, a village in the p. of Dirleton, East Lothian. Pop. 201.
FENTON, a village of St. Louis co., in the state of Missouri, U. S., 137 m. E of Jefferson city, on the Merrimac river.
FENTON-CULVERT, or GREAT FENTON, a township in the p. of Stoke-upon-Trent, Staffordshire, 23 m. ESE of Newcastle-under-Lyne, intersected by the Manchester and Birmingham railway. Pop. 3,744.

FENTON-KIRK, a parish and township in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. SSE of Tadcaster, 10 m. 56 chains railway distance SSW of York, and close upon the York and North Midland line. Area of p., 4,410 acres. Pop. 608. Area of township, 2,160 acres. Pop. 378. It includes also the township of Little Fenton. Pop. 104.
FENTON-VIVIAN, or LITTLE FENTON, a township in the p. of Stoke-upon-Trent, Staffordshire, 21/4 m. E of Newcastle-under-Lyne. Pop. 1,179. 18,722.
FENTONVILLE, a village of Fenton township, Genesee co., in the state of Michigan, U. S., 57 m. NNW of Detroit. Pop. of township, 660.
FENTRESS, a county in the N part of the state FER of Tennessee, U. S., comprising an area of 560 sq. m., watered by branches of Obey's river, and White Oak creek. Pop. in 1840, 3,550.

FENWICK, a township in the parochial chapelry of Kyloe, co. of Durham. Pop. 227.—Also a township in the p. of Stamfordham, Northumberland, 131 m. WNW of Newcastle-on-Tyne, on a branch of the Pont. Pop. 66.—Also a township in the p. of Campsall, W. R. of Yorkshire, 51 m. SW of Snaith, and S of the Went. Area 2,060 acres. Pop. 262.
FENWICK, a parish of Ayrshire, 4 m. NE of Kilmarnock. Pop. 2,018.
FENWICK (UPPER), a village in the p. of Fenwick, Ayrshire. Pop. 355.
FEN-Y, or Fun-E, a district and town of China. in the prov. of Keang-se, dep. and 20 m. E of Yuenchoo, on the l. bank of the Yu-ho, an affluent of the Kan-kiang.
FENY-NAGY, a town of Hungary, in the comitat of Bacs, 10 m. S of Theresianopol.

FEOCK (Saint), a parish in Cornwall, 41 m. S of Truro, bounded on the E by the Truro, and on the S by Falmouth harbour. The Redruth and Chasewater railway terminates at Point Quay in this p. Area 2,530 acres. Pop. 1,476.
FEODOSIA. See KAFFA.
FEOU, or Fou, a town and district of China, in the prov. of Shen-see, on the r. bank of the Lo-ho, 41 m. S of Yen-ngan.
FEOU-CHAN, a town and district of China, in the prov. of Shan-see, dep. and 30 m. ESE of Pinyang, on an affluent of the Yuen-ho.
FERAGE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, cant. of Hulsonnieaux. Pop. 62.

FERASHEESH, a district of Tunis, near the W confines of the regency, and to the S of the 35th parallel. It is bordered on the N by the ranges of the Jebel-Shaambi and Shamani, and intersected by some of the upper streams of the Wady Shivayah.
FERBANE, a town in King's co., 5½ m. SW of Ballycumber, on the river Brosna, in a flat marshy district. Pop. in 1831, 501; in 1841, 537.
FERBANNA, a town of Bambuk, in W. Africa, 40 m. NW of Bambuk, on the Sanon-Koley.
FERBANNA-TENDA, a town of W. Africa, in the state of Dentilia, on the W bank of the Falemé, 65 m. SW of Bambuk.
FERCULA, a district of Africa, in the N part of the Sahara, bordering on Tafilet. It has some measure of fertility, and carries on some trade. Hartmann supposes it to be the Vareklan of Edrissi.
FERDINAND (SAINT), a township and village of St. Louis co., in the state of Louisiana, U. S., about 14 m. NW of St. Louis. Pop. 3,079.
FERDINANDO (SANTO), a city of Chili, the cap. of the prov. of Colchagua, situated near the river Tinguiririca, in S lat. 34° 18'.
FERDRUFT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vosges, cant. of Ramonchamp. Pop. 1,200.

FERE (LA), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Aisne, on an island in the Oise, near the confluence of the Oise and Serre, 14 m. NW of Laon, and 90 m. N by E of Paris. Pop. 3,129. It stands in a marshy district, and was strongly fortified by Cardinal Mazarine, but is now only surrounded by a wall flanked with towers, and protected by two half-moons. It has a large arsenal, and a school of artillery. An action took place here between the French and the allies on 1st March, 1814.—The cant. comprises 26 com. Pop. in 1841,

FERE-CHAMPENOISE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Marne, between the Seine and the Marne, 20 m. S of Epernay. It was the scene of an action between the French and the Allies on the 25th March, 1814.—The cant. comprises 20 com. Pop. in 1841, 7,675.

FERE-EN-TARDENOIS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Aisne, 14 m. NNE of Chateau-Thierry. It has an active trade in cattle, grain, wood, wine, and lint. Pop. 2,212.—The cant. comprises 23 com. Pop. in 1841, 12,113.
FEREH, or FEREHJIK, a town of Turkey, in Romelia, in the sanjak of Gallipoli, on the Maritza, below Ipsalah. Pop. 3,000. It is the ancient Berhaa. There are hot springs in the neighbourhood.
FEREKHABAD. See FURUCKHABAD.
FEREL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, 24 m. SE of Vannes. Pop. 1,489.
FEREOL-DES-COTES (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. and 2 m. SW of Ambert. Pop. 1,295.
FERENTILLO, a town of the Papal states, in the deleg. of Spoleto. Pop. 2,054.

FERENTINO, a small antique town of the Papal states, in the Campagna-di-Roma, 78 m. SE of Rome, 5 m. NW of Frosinone, on an eminence commanding an extensive view of the plain towards Frosinone on the one side, and Anagni and Rome on the other. Pop. 7,200. Ferentinum is mentioned by Strabo as a city belonging to the Samnites. The present town occupies the same site, and presents many curious specimens of rude Cyclopean architecture. It is likewise rich in classical remains, of which some account is given in the 2d vol. of Sir R. C. Hoare's Classical Tour.
FEREOLLE (Sainte), a commune of France, in the dep. of Corrèze, cant. of Donzenac. Pop. 2,826.
FERERE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Brabant, cant. of Bousval. Pop. 117.
FERERIAS, a small town of the island of Majorca.
FERESINA (CANALE DI), a narrow channel of the gulf of Venice, between Istria and the island of Cherso. It forms part of the large bay of Quarnero.
FEREZ, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Albacete, partido of Yeste, to the W of one of the Sierrasde-Ciezar. It has a parish-church and an hospital.
FERFERUN. See FERAFREH.
FERGA (AL), a town of Arabia, in the prov. of Hedjaz, 5 m. SSE of Medina.
FERGANAH. See KHOKAN.

FERGUS, the principal river of co. Clare. It rises in the barony of Corcomroe, and runs to the head of the estuary of the F., a brief distance below the v. of Clare, and has an entire run to Clare of about 20 m., the first half of which is prevailingly E, and the second half prevailingly S. It forms or traverses Loughs Inchiquin, Tedane, Dromore, Ballyally, and several other lakes; draws down affluents from the borders of co. Galway, and from the uplands in co. Clare; and washes the town of Ennis, where it is increased by the Clareen. From Ennis to Clare, it resembles in appearance and character a large canal; and has a medium width of 150 ft., and a medium depth of about 14 ft. in summer, and from 18 to 25 ft. during the prevalence of rains. The estuary of the F. commences a 1 m. below the bridge of Clare; and has a length S to the Shannon of 6 m., with a breadth somewhat regularly increasing from mere river-limits at the head to 5 m. across the entrance. The channel possesses good anchoring grounds in from 3 to 6 faths. of water. Vessels of 16 ft. draught can safely navigate the channel, and may, at all times, ground without damage on its mud banks.
FERGUS, a village of Upper Canada, in Nichol township, on the Grand river, 13 m. from Guelph. Pop. 180.
FERGUS (SAINT), a maritime parish in the co. of Banff, 4 m. W of Peterhead. The coast is bold and rocky. Pop. in 1801, 1,270; in 1841, 1,391. FER
FERGUSHILL, a village in the p. of Kilwinning, Ayrshire. Pop. 279.

FERGUSON, a township of Centre co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 11 m. S of Bellefonte, drained by Spring creek, and its affluents. It is intersected by several ridges of mountains. The soil in the valleys consists of calcareous loam. Pop. in 1840, 1,755.
FERHABAD. See FEHRABAD.
FERHAD, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Khuzistan, 40 m. SE of Nishapur.
FERIA, a small town of Spanish Estremadura, on an abrupt mountain near the Guadajera, 30 m. SSE of Badajos. Pop. 6,000.

FERIANA, or FERRIANAH, a town of Tunis, in the district of Ferasheesh, on the r. side of the Wady- Bon-Atayah, 130 m. SW of Tunis. Dr. Shaw supposes this place to mark the site of the ancient Thala. About 20 m. to the E are the ruins of the ancient Medina-al-Kadima, and an aqueduct.
FERICSANCZE, a town of Austria, in Slavonia, in the subdivision of Verocze, 35 m. W of Esseg, on an affluent of the Drau.

FERIDUN, a district of Persia, in the prov. of Irak-Ajemi, stretching along the W declivities of the mountains behind Kunsar, and along a beautiful valley in the Zagros chain. It is inhabited by a colony of Georgians and Armenians.—Kinneir.
FERINGABAD, a village in Beluchistan, 6 m. NE of Mustung, in N lat. 29° 50'. Pop. 800.
FERINGAL, a rich lead-mine in Afghanistan, in the valley and 18 m. SW of Ghorbund, in about N lat. 34° 55'.—Lord.
FERINGI-BAZAR, a town of Bengal, in the district of Jelalpur, on the W side of the Dalasesi, in N lat. 23° 33'. It was founded, in 1666, by a colony of Portuguese fugitives from Aracan.
FERINGI-PETTAH, a small town of Hindostan, in S. Canara, 10 m. N of Mangalor. It was devastated by Tippu in 1783.
FERLA, a town of Sicily, in the district of Noto, 18 m. NW of Syracuse.

FERLACH (UPPER and Lower), two villages of Carinthia, in the circle and 6 m. S of Klagenfurt, on the Drave. In the latter there is a large manufactory of fire-arms, carried on for the Austrian government. Pop. of the two, 2,000.

FERMANAGH, an inland county of Ulster, bounded, on the N, by Donegal and Tyrone; on the NE, by Tyrone; on the E, by Tyrone and Monaghan; on the SE, by Monaghan; on the S and SW, by Cavan; and on the W, by Cavan and Leitrim. Its outline is proximately that of an oblong, extending from ESE to WNW. Its greatest length is 353 m.; its greatest breadth is 193 m.; and its area comprises 289,228 acres of arable land, 114,847 of uncultivated land, 6,155 of continuous plantations, 210 of towns, and 46,755 of water: in all, 457,195 acres. Surface.] The co., from extremity to extremity, is bisected NW or a little transversely, by Upper Lough Erne, Lower Lough Erne, and the short run of river and meadowy chain of ponds and loughlets which connect them; and as all its waters are collected into the Erne, it necessarily forms a vast trough, whose sides somewhat regularly decline toward each other, and whose bottom is occupied by a broad band of water. The Cuilcagh or Dowbally mountain, in the SE division, the loftiest summit in the co., attains an alt. of 2,188 ft. In the NW division the principal summits, named in order on the NW, are Aghamore, alt. 1,249 ft.; Oramore, 854 ft.; Slapragh, 846 ft.; Belmore, 1,312 ft.; Glenkeel, 1,223 ft.; Knockmore, 919 ft.; Drumbad, 1,009 ft.; Blackslee, 1,026 ft.; East Shean, 1,030 ft.; North Shean, 1,175 ft.; Bolusty, 1,064 ft.; Tiranagher, 875 ft.; and $ Glenalong, 793 ft. About two-thirds of all the SE district are nearly champaign or but gently variegated,-and the chief heights of the whole region are Glenvannan and Tappaghan, with alts. of respectively 730 ft. and 1,110 ft. Rivers.] The streams of the co., additional to the Erne, are numerous, but generally unimportant. The chief are the Clodagh, the Arney, and the Sillies, on the SW; the curious subterranean Roogagh to Lough Melvin; and the Finn, the Drumany, and the Ballycassidy, on the NE. Lower Lough Macnean, though partly on the boundary with Cavan, lies chiefly within Fermanagh; Upper Lough Macnean lies on the boundary with Cavan and Leitrim; Lough Melvin separates an extreme NW tongue of the co. from Leitrim. Other lakes on the boundaries, and especially in the interior, are almost multitudinous, but rarely have larger dimensions than those of loughlets or ponds. Geology.] Carboniferous limestone zones all Upper Lough Erne, flanks all the middle Erne, lies round by far the greater part of Lower Lough Erne, and extends away in broad expanses to the county's boundaries. Yellow sandstone and conglomerate occupy a district of about 36 sq. m. around Tempo, and two districts of each about 15 sq. m. respectively NE of Belmore mountain, and along the shore of Lough Melvin. Millstone grit, with its accompanying formations, occupies a district of about 55 sq m. between the two smaller yellow sandstone districts. Old red sandstone, and sandstone conglomerate, occupy a district of 13 m. in length from N to S, and extending in breadth from the boundary of the co. W to the margin of the upper part of Lower Lough Erne, and the side of the valley or low ground of the middle Erne.—In 1841, the continuous plantations within the co. amounted to 6,155 acres; and the grand total of woodland surface was 7,802 acres. Agriculture.] The soil of the sandstone and grit upland districts is naturally cold and moorish; but has, to a large extent in the aggregate, and very generally in the arable grounds, been much ameliorated. The soil of the limestone declivities, or of the lands of medium elevation within the limestone district, is, for the most part, a good brown loam, lying upon a moderately retentive subsoil, and capable of being rendered extremely fertile under both green and cereal crops. Much of the soil of the low grounds on both sides of the bottom of the grand central valley, is a rich loam on a clayey subsoil, and occasionally on peat; and portions are so moist and spongy as to be constantly disposed in meadows and pasture. The farms vary in size from 5 to 50 Irish acres; and average about 10 or 12 acres. The proportion in tillage is two-thirds; the rent of the larger farms is £1 10s. per acre; and that of the smaller ones from £1 15s., to £2 and upwards, per acre. In 1841, the number of farms of from 1 acre to 5 acres was 7,381; of from 5 to 15 acres, 7,543; of from 15 to 30 acres, 1,696; and of upwards of 30 acres, 529. In the same year, the number of farmers was 13,300; of servants and labourers, 21,858. -The number of acres under crop in 1848 was 102,425; of which 7,598 were under wheat; 41,193 under oats; 5,974 under barley, bere, and rye; 1,087 under pease and beans; 9,185 under potatoes; 4,606 under turnips; 1,481 under mangel-wurzel and other roots; 1,131 under flax; and 30,170 under clover.- In 1841, the live stock, together with their respective value, were, 6,517 horses and mules, £52,136; 2,991 asses, £2,991; 50,975 cattle, £331,337; 8,130 sheep, £8,943; 21,365 pigs, £26,706; and 163,841 poultry, £4,096. Grand total of value of live stock in the rural districts, £426,209.—The trade of the co., in nearly all departments, is so generally concentrated in Enniskillen, that a succinct statement of it here would be little else than a repetition of what is said in the article on the town. The trade in butter is considerable; and coarse linen is made to some extent.—The facilities of communication by the Erne and the Ulster canal are noticed in Tesino, all of which fall into the Adriatic. Corn. III. * FER the article on Enniskillen.-The county inspector had under his charge, in 1842, 868 m. of road. The principal lines of road in the county radiate from Enniskillen, and lead toward respectively Sligo, Ballyshannon, Pettigo, Omagh, Clogher, Monaghan, Cavan, and Swanlinbar. Towns and Divisions.] The towns are Enniskillen, Irvinestown, and Maguire's Bridge; and the chief villages are Rosslea, Newton-Butler, Belleek, Ederney, Kesh, Lisnaskea, Tempo, Brookborough, Lisbellaw, and Ballinamallard.—The co. is divided into 8 baronies, and 23 parishes.—Dr. Beaufort, stating the number of parishes and churches in 1792 at respectively 18 and 26, says that 3 ps. and 3 churches are in the dio. of Kilmore, and 15 ps. and 23 churches in the dio. of Clogher. Statistics.] In 1824 the number of schools was 240, of scholars 9,793; of scholars connected with the Established church, 5,139; of scholars connected with the Roman Catholic body, 4,424. In 1841, the children attending school were returned at 7,855, of whom 228 were attending superior schools. In Sept. 1848, the National society schools in this co. were 124, attended by 10,214 children.—The total number of offenders, in 1841, was 390; and of these 85 were committed for offences against the person.-The co. sends 2 members to the imperial parliament. Constituency in 1841, 2,271; in 1849, 914.—Pop. in 1831, 149,763; in 1841, 156,481. Males, 76,982; females, 79,499; families, 28,654. Inhabited houses, 26,796; uninhabited houses, 1,005. Families residing in first-class houses, 292; in second-class houses, 6,409; in third-class houses, 11,951; in fourth-class houses, 10,002. Families employed chiefly in agriculture, 20,665; in manufactures and trade, 6,127; in other pursuits, 1,862. History.] F. was constituted a co. in the 11th year of Elizabeth, and was not reduced to a state of fair order at "the Plantation of Ulster" in the time of James I. The forfeitures consequent on the rebellion of 1641 sensibly altered the social phases of the county, and considerably increased the possessions of the English and Scottish settlers. The historical events of any note occurred at the epochs of that rebellion and of the war of the Revolution, and are glanced at in the article on Enniskillen.—In 1703, John Verney, the descendant of a very ancient family, long settled in Buckinghamshire, was created Baron Verney of Belturbet, and Viscount Fermanagh.
FERMANVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. and 2 m. NW of Saint- Pierre-Eglise. Pop. 1,967.
FERME (SAINT), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. and 4 m. S of Pellegrue. Pop. 930.
FERMEDO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarca and 30 m. WNW of Aveiro, and 11 m. SSW of Oporto, near the shore of the Atlantic.

FERMETE' (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nièvre, cant. and 5 m. SW of Saint- Benin-d'Azy, 9 m. ESE of Nevers, on the r. bank of the Ixeur. It has an iron-work, and quarries of mill-stone.

FERMO, a delegation and town of the Pontifical states. The delegazione or administrative prov. is bounded on the NW by the deleg. of Macerata; on the E by the Adriatic; on the S and SW by the deleg. of Ascoli; and on the W by that of Camerino. Its greatest length from N to S is 29 m., and its extreme breadth about 20 m., comprising a superficial area of 248 sq. m. of 60 to a degree. Pop. in 1833, 89,404; in 1844, 104,116. It is subdivided into 7 governi and 47 comuni, viz.: Fermo, Grottamare, Monte-Giorgio, Monte - Rubbiano, Ripatransone, Santo - Elpidio-a- Mare, and Santa-Vittoria. It is flat near the coast, but several ramifications of the Apennines penetrate its western districts. The principal streams by which it is watered are the Chienti, Tenna, Leta, Aso, and maize, and silk, form the chief productions of the soil. The rearing of cattle and of bees, and fishing, constitute also important branches of local industry. —The town of F., the cap. of the deleg., is situated at the foot of a hill between the Tenna and Leta, at an alt. of 940 ft. above sea-level, 4 m. from the Adriatic, 36 m. SSE of Ancona, and 117 m. NNE of Rome. It is enclosed by a wall, and contains a cathedral, several churches and convents, a university, and a gallery of painting and sculpture. It has a small but well-frequented port, and carries on a considerable trade in grain, silk, and woollen fabrics. The foundation of this town is supposed to have been anterior to that of Rome, and is generally attributed to the Sabines. It is noted also as the birthplace of Lactantius and Galeas-Sforce.
FERMOSA, a lake of Brazil, in the S part of the prov. of Goyaz, in which the Maranhão, a branch of the Tocantins, takes its rise.

FERMOSELLE, a fortified frontier town of Spain, in the prov. and 40 m. SW of Zamora, partido of Bermillo-de-Savago, on a hill, near the r. bank of the Duero, and confluence of the Tormes. Pop. 3,360. It has a parish-church, and a custom-house; and possesses extensive manufactories of linen.

FERMOY, a parish and town in co. Cork. Area of p. 3,481 acres. Pop. in 1831, 7,839; in 1841, 7,513. The surface extends along the Blackwater.—The post and market-town of F., and a grand military station, on the road from Dublin to Cork, by way of Clonmel, is 21 m. SSW of Kilworth; 141 m. E of Mallow; and 171 m. NNE of Cork. Though a comparatively ancient seat of pop., F. appears never, till within the last half-century, to have risen much above hamletimportance. In 1796, when the estate on which it stands was purchased by the late John Anderson, Esq., from one of the co-heiresses of the Forward family, the v. consisted merely of a carman's inn and a few wretched cabins, and stood amidst grounds clumped over with furze and in a general state of neglect. F. is a remarkably pretty and prettily situated place. It lies on the r. bank of the Blackwater, and from its extending in a line along the river à la militaire, and having a hollow square in the centre of the line in front of the bridge, covered too both in flank and rear by fine cultivated wooded slopes, it presents a more imposing appearance than most other towns of the same size. The principal part of the town occupies the lower grounds; the sides and summits of the hills are covered and diversified with villas and plantations. The barracks crown the heights on the l. bank of the river; and from their elevation and magnitude, form a remarkable feature for many miles around. The barracks form two distinct squares, and contain accommodation for infantry and cavalry to the amount of jointly about 3,000 men. The bridge which crosses the Blackwater at the town is a stone structure of 13 arches, built in 1689.—The manufactures introduced by Mr. Anderson included coach-making, paper-making, flourmaking, and brewing; but, viewed in the aggregate, they have not been successful. The trade of the town consists principally in the retail supply of general goods to the military and the surrounding gentry, and in the exchange of a few articles for the agricultural produce of an extensive surrounding district. Pop. of the town in 1831, 6,976; in 1841, 6,379. Houses 808. Families employed chiefly in agriculture, 366; in manufactures and trade, 574; in other pursuits, 304.
FERMOYLE, a village in the p. of Killonoghan, co. Clare. Pop. in 1831, 220.
